title: 2026/02/11 - GA [Part 3]
displayTitle: 2026-02-11 - Governance and Administration Committee - Emergency Management Bill [Part 3]
description: 2 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Emergency Management Bill (No 2) Hon Mark Mitchell, Minister for Emergency Management Aotearoa Community Resilience Network Rodney Barber, Chair Local Government New Zealand [Zoom] New Zealand Veterinary Association Kevin Bryant, Chief Executive Officer Max Mashall Federated Farmers of New Zealand Sandra Faulkner, National Board Member and Adverse Events Spokesperson
descriptionSummary: Emergency Management Bill. Emergency Management Bill (No 2). Hon Mark Mitchell, Minister for Emergency Management

chunks.text: Get a speaking list going to Publicly, Hopefully.

Welcome Minister on the road. What a leadership. What a what A busy minister leadership. Sorry. God. I, sorry, I just dropped you. Um, yeah, we, We, we thought you'd rolled the car. We saw that, um, it was, um, rolling and not the car. Um, thank you for joining us today, um, again, and we appreciate you making yourself available.

I understand you're traveling in, um, uhor somewhere today. So thanks for, um, keeping this, uh, appointment. Uh, we had just heard from the, uh, NEMA officials on the, the bill, and you're our first submitter as the, as the minister moving this bill. So we do have 15 minutes with you.

Uh, people will have, I think, have some questions, but would you like to just make a few minutes of opening points on, on this bill winner? No. First of all, I just wanna thank you Madam Chair and also the committee for allowing me the flexibility of, um, being able to call in like this and not be there in person because, um, you know, that that is very much appreciated.

Um, look, I'm conscious of the fact that we've only got 15 minutes, so I don't wanna waste your time. I'd rather just go straight to, um, any questions that you might have. I understand that, um, you know, the, the Nima, Dave and the team and the officials have been in front of you answering any questions. If you've got anything for me, um, you know, just fire away. Thank you. Um, I'll just start off, if you don't mind minister with, um, one of the key objectives as stated in the book, bill, is the role of Iwi Maori.

And I know that you've commented on the, uh, integral role that, um, Marai in particular play during emergencies. Um, we do have, uh, an ability in this bill for, um, local representatives to be included at the planning stages. And I just, I wanted to get your feedback on that and whether you would like the committee to look at, um, the sufficiency of that, um, provision in the bill, or if there's anything else you were looking for us to particularly, uh, focus on during the submission process in that area.

Yeah, I, yeah, that's a really good point to raise. Look, the only thing that I'd say to the committee is that, um, you know, you should have a very open mind to any use submissions that are brought forward as a result of the, um, tropical low that we just had come through. And obviously it's been pretty devastating for us as a country with the loss of nine people and still searching for one.

Um, and so there may be some fresh submissions come through, um, as a result of that, um, that may mean that we can make some changes. So I know Madam Chair, that, um, with your level of engagement on this, that, um, and I'm sure the committee will, will support you that, um, if there are some good submissions that come forward that need to be looked at and could strengthen the bill, then, you know, let's do that.

Um, in relation to the KE yes, that I've, I've had many conversations with a lot of the iwi, both the Iwi impacted, uh, with the weather event that came through right from Northland through to the, um, east Cape and um, and also with engagement with the Iwi leaders at, um, at Yang.

And that there's been really good, um, conversations had there and lots of support for what we're doing with the bill and the fact that it's critically important. They do have a seat, um, at the table, which of course we codify in this legislation. Thank you. And thank you for invitation to, uh, look at the submissions.

We, um, have already asked the team if there's any areas to focus on for us, and they said that they'll get back to us on that too. So we are keen to, uh, make some proactive contributions as well. I'll just check if there's anyone else wants to ask. Yes, yep. I've go to Andy Foster. Thank you Minister. Um, Mark, great to see you out on the job and thank you for the work you're doing.

Um, the, thanks Anne. The question I'm just following up on is just one of, I have asked the officials here, but um, I know I've al also asked you, um, is just in the bill, is there enough around making sure that there's an interface between a reduction from an emergency management point of view? So we try and avoid the emergency in the first place.

Um, and things like planning in particularly, you know, you're talking about land use and RMA stuff. Is there enough there that actually makes sure that's gonna happen? We stop building dumb things in dumb places? Well, that, that's captured in the fact that, um, you know, district and, and regional councils have to provide plans around, um, exactly that, around risk management and, uh, and, uh, mitigation.

So that's a, that's a definite change from where we have been in the past. Well, I think it's definitely been strengthened in the bill. Um, but at the end of the day, Andy, you, you know, the, the issue that you raise is a critical one for us, the country. It's a really difficult and complex one when you're talking about managed retreat. Um, you know, certainly around existing communities, but, um, and, and from my experience, both district and regional councils are very sensitive to that.

And without a doubt there's a fresh focus and energy that's been brought to making sure that there's serious planning go into it. That's excellent. Um, look, and the other area if I can is just, uh, the location of, of emergency equipment at a, at a high level. I was on the West coast, um, uh, just before Christmas. They were very anxious about where particularly things like Bailey Bridges, 'cause they've got a lot of rivers there.

We're located, which at the moment they're all in Canterbury in the South island and Hawkes Bay in the north. Similarly, we, we have, uh, issues around, uh, the East Cape in particular, um, just thought to going, where going locating really critical emergency equipment storage of, of vital facilities like, um, fuel, et cetera. And I know we've got lifelines, um, covered.

Have we got those well enough being thought through by the relevant agencies? The short answer is yes. Um, but there's always an emergency management, there's always ongoing work and improvement. Um, that's just the nature of the type of work that they do, that everyone does. Um, the, the Prime Minister himself has had a big focus on what equipment do we have and where is it placed.

So there is some work being done at DPMC on that. Um, and uh, yeah, so no, there's, there's, um, there's, there is some work being done around that. And the other, the other point I was gonna make, Andy, is that the paper that I'm taking relates directly to how do we better prepare ourselves and where do we make the investment, um, as a country.

Yeah, look, so I think we'd be, we very much benefit from getting a little bit more clarity about some of those decisions which you made at DPMC, which obviously was aligned much more with, um, NEMA before, um, it went to DIA. So, no, appreciate that. Thank you. Thank you, Tim. Thanks, um, minister, nice to see you. Hey, you mentioned in your opening comments about wanting to really listen carefully to submitters, particularly based on recent events.

Um, and I'm just wondering, is there anything that you have seen, I know you've been really active in the response to this as you have been across a num well, e every, uh, sort of civil emergency we've seen. Is there anything that you've seen in the last three weeks that has maybe gives you, uh, cause to think we should be looking at particular changes here?

Or are you still pretty comfortable with where it's at? I think the only thing that I would probably highlight to the committee and something that you could have a look at to see whether or not these speak kind of formalized through the, through the, uh, legislation is that great barrier island, which of course is part of, um, Auckland Council area, um, they get hit particularly hard.

They tend to get hit with the weather more consistent with what the Corman or these Cape gets. And, um, one of the issues that cinema arose through this event was, um, should they have the, the ability to be able to declare, um, you know, to a, a very specific declaration for them Oh yeah. Rather the entire area. So, um, I don't know whether that requires any change legislation, but, um, if the committee wants something to have a look at, I I'll point them in that direction.

Thank you very much. Thanks Simon. Just, uh, minister, we just have some, um, fire dancing outside parliament here, so you might might be coming through a little bit. Um, I can't, yeah. Thank you minister. Um, just obviously the changes is gonna put, um, I guess additional costs onto local authorities at a time when there's obviously pressure to reduce rates as it is.

Has there been any discussions around, um, how that can be avoided or cost recoveries So that work has all been done? Um, we, we were very sensitive to that, that the new legislation didn't pass on, um, enormous costs to, uh, local government. And there's been a lot of work done by the outstanding NEMA officials that have worked on this legislation and, uh, working with local government.

Yeah, Thank you. And that was actually, I was gonna follow up that with that question too. So it is, um, I, I think something that we often hear is, is there enough, um, provision for local government, they've got so many, um, so many different things that they need to pay for and, you know, will they have enough, a lot, enough money to actually be able to fulfill their obligations under this legislation?

So I think that's something we'll be looking to submit. As for, as well, the other area that we briefly touched on, um, was the interrelationship between the, um, resource management area of adaptation and this bill, um, and the team has advised us that they have been in touch with some of the people doing that work, um, in other parts of government.

But do you see that this bill can interrelate with the, uh, work that's happening on adaptation effectively? Because that's gonna be a key, um, area of preparedness? Look, to be honest with you, Madam Dear Beth, and the, um, and our policy team are much better to talk to that in detail to you, um, my expectation is the adaptation work is gonna continue.

Um, that's gonna take, you know, it's a big piece of complicated work. Obviously it is gonna have impacts on this legislation and, um, whether or not we've got the flexibility inside the bill to be, because you don't know what you don't know. Um, but the, the reality of it is if the, if the bill, if it can't make the changes, then obviously there are other mechanisms and ways of, of being able to make sure any future work around adaptation is, and it needs to be captured inside our emergency management bill.

There are ways of doing that. You very much Yeah, well that's useful. That, that, that does say do something else. Yeah. Did You No, no, I, yeah, I think I'm okay with That. Yeah. Okay. Um, okay. I think that's, um, that's about all the questions we have. We're dealing with, um, some noise here at our end, and obviously you are in the, the car there, so we won't, um, won't, um, prolong, uh, the submission any longer.

But I think really helpful to get that, um, guidance for you around the areas. And we are looking to the team too, if there are other areas that they want us to focus on as a committee, because we are really keen to make sure that this is the best piece of legislation that we can have. And I appreciate the comment around Great Barrier Island, and I noticed, um, I think we had, um, rota City Council having some concerns around the, the state of emergency that was declared as well.

So that's certainly an area we can take forward. Yeah, I went on out there, um, Madam Chair, I had Adam Mags with me and, um, they're already working and the, um, chair of the local board out there and, and so there, there's already some work being done on that, um, to see whether or not we can sort of fix it in the short term.

But, you know, it might, the committee might identify that we can fix that in the medium to longer term. And, and, you know, and, and even in a broader context, because the thing about our weather events that we have in our country is that you can have this bizarre situation where half the town is severely impacted and the other half is BAU. Um, you know, so there's, so I think the system's gotta have a bit of flexibility to be able to reflect that as well.

Yeah. Okay. Just one final question. Andy. Andy's got one final Question. Yeah, sorry, mark on, on that one. Um, I mean, I can remember the only state of emergency, which we actually had declared in Wellington, apart from COVID, um, was one that I declared for the south coast of Wellington. It was only the south coast because of storm swell. So we did isolate that particular area and see that's the only area we're interested in. We're not interested in anything else.

Um, and that was the only area we, so we did have the flexibility to do that. Yes, you can do that. And that has, that happened in the South Island recently as well. So I know that there was a mechanism in there for that. Um, it's just that the committee asked if there was another area to have a look at as a result of what happened during the, um, and I just, I'm just pointing you to that.

Um, and, and the other thing is just no, seriously, if you guys spot anything, we see anything in those submissions that you think we should, we can strengthen the bill, then, you know, let's do that. I think for me it was very much that tie, which I think you were alluding to between, um, between the emergency management side and things like, um, uh, adaptation and, um, mitigation.

That way, if we can do anything to strengthen it that way, I think it's, that's a good thing to do. Yeah, that'd be good. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Well, thank you. We are at time now, minister. So thank you very much again for joining us. And, um, the team, um, of officials here have been doing a great job. And also just to, uh, reinforce, we acknowledged, um, the terrible situation at the beginning of this year, um, Mount, uh, Nui and Moo in particular, um, part as part of our private sessions.

It's just to, um, say that, that, um, we are are very much, uh, that that's very much at the forefront of our mind as we work through this legislation. Yeah, Absolutely. Thanks Madam Chair. Thank you everyone. Thank you. Thanks. Bye. See you. Bye. See ya. Thank you. Uh, so we'll now come to our next submitter who is the Community Resilience Network, and I think we have, uh, them Rodney.
Rodney, yeah. Welcome, uh, to, uh, come and join us, please. Yeah. Yep.

Thank you, uh, very much for that. We've got 10 minutes for your submission. We have received your written, uh, submission. Uh, and so if you wanna make some opening comments, and then we'll move to questions from members. Okay. Over to you. Okay. I've just got two minute introduction. That's perfectly fine. Yep, yep. Okay. You can actually use the whole time if you wish to speak.

Yeah, I understand that. It's up to you. So, um, thank you for the opportunity to, to speak today. Appreciate that. Um, our submission is about one simple but critical idea that disaster resilience cannot be built directly. It's the outcome of strong community resilience, uh, which we define as economic, social, cultural, and environmental.

The government acquired to North Island severe weather events makes clear the most serious failures occurred before and during the early days of response when communities were largely left to cope on their own where outcomes were better. It was not because the formal system arrived sooner because community and iwi networks already active, connected, and trusted.

The work underway in Newlands has been designed deliberately around that reality. Our starting assumption is that large scale or multi reg region events, government systems will not be able to meet all the local needs immediately. Planning therefore, focuses on ensuring communities can self-organize and reduce harm during the first five to seven days.

The period the inquiry shows is most vulnerable. This is operationalized through sustained investment in relationships and trust, clearly defined community governance and operational roles, ongoing training, mentoring and professional supervision, shared awareness of local vulnerabilities and assets and simple practice mechanisms for neighbor to neighbor support.

Importantly, this work does not replace councils CDM groups or national agencies. It strengthens the overall system by ensuring communities are functioning before the system is under stress. Distress, allowing councils and agencies to focus on infrastructure coordination and recovery at scale. This gives practical effect, the inquiry's recommendation to recognize people and communities as an active fourth pillar of emergency management.

In short, the inquiry course for urgent system change, the Newlands pilot demonstrates how its recommendations could be translated into practical action by putting community resilience first and designing explicitly for the first seven days. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, we'll just come to questions now.

I just had one, I agree with you that, um, community, um, resilience is essential. Do you think that there is enough in this bill to promote, um, community resilience? Um, I think the idea is there, what we suggested in our written submission was acknowledging that more formally than the legislation You, it, um, Andy, did you Sure.

I have to go back and have a look at what it says in the legislation, but, um, Rodney, it strikes me over the years, and I'm most familiar with Wellington, obviously good to see it, um, most familiar with Wellington, obviously that we've been a bit like this. It's been a sort of bit of a rollercoaster in terms of the engagement between the authorities of getting a nod as well, the engagement between the authorities and the councils, et cetera, and the community in terms of the expectations, the support of, um, the community to be prepared.

Um, does that ring true for you that it's, it's not been consistent, it's been up and down, Um, in the six years it's taken to get to, to this point, it's been statically down, consistently down, which is why we made a decision as a board on the 3rd of July last year to instead of raise investment to fund us, we're trying to catalyze 300 residents to collectively implement this over 18 months so that we are doing it as a community without external support.

So I was just wondering in terms of the, the question I was gonna ask about the bill is whether there is, because if we, if we put a, uh, an onus on the authorities at each different level, whether it's nationally or, or locally or regionally, um, to monitor that level of community engagement, to require that support and, and engagement, and then to monitor, um, that, and that includes personal preparedness, but also the community preparedness.

When you measure, you tend to get, when you measure, you tend to get engagement of the authorities. When you don't measure, it gets outta sight, outta mind. Is that is a reasonable, reasonable thing to, to, I guess our response to that would be we're developing this sustainable model and we've been measuring community and disaster resilience for six years now using the treasury living standards framework lined up to the government's resilience framework.

So we are measuring that to identify the vulnerabilities data, keeping the trend lines up to 2030, static at best, declining at worst, which to us just isn't good enough. Thank You. Laga, Thank, which is why we're doing what we're doing. We, we are part of the solution and we think it's complimentary to what's being discussed here.

Um, it's been hard work, but we've got a sense of momentum. Yeah. So thank you. So just actually following on from Andy's question In terms of the community resilience, 'cause uh, you talk in your submission and your written submission, the readiness is social investment. So obviously every community it would be that they're ready because when something happens, you're ready to go.

Can you, um, elaborate, uh, on the community re resilience, because that's sort of like a period of time and do you think some of that is covered in the bill? It'll probably more of a local level as, but an overarching sort of theme around community resilience as a partner to emergency funders?

Yeah, I mean, I, I think if we do our job properly at community level, it's going to be better for everyone including the emergency responders because we are well connected on the ground. Um, we are seeing community resilience as building social economic and cultural cohesion across 14,000 people in one suburb.

And that's our main focus calendar 2026. And then in about March next year, we would start six months disaster resilience model that we've developed in consultation with Remo because we don't, we think the community needs, there needs to be high trust connections and cohesion culturally, socially, and economically before we think it's worth implementing the model we've developed.

And we think that would take six months next year. And in the meantime, other suburbs are approaching us to talk about how they could adopt and adapt it and it would be free. And just two quick questions. Do you have a connection into young people in your area and also diverse? Oh My gosh, the quick, um, yes.

Okay. Through the intermediate and the college. Yeah. Um, I think that your idea is really aligned with where a lot of the, uh, work, especially the Remo work has been in terms of their, um, discussions around community resilience and you know, how they have, um, specifically ideas around even seeing Wellington as a, as a series of islands after an emergency.

And so that seems really consistent with that. Um, But we've, sorry, we've seen the need to do more than they offer Yeah, yeah. Is actually a ground, a groundswell and some momentum amongst residents. Yeah. So does that, so in terms of what they're doing to, do you, you see what you're doing as compliment, you don't see them as stopping work that needs to be done in your area, very much fix, Correct. We see them as supporting us in some areas Yeah. And what we're doing.

Okay. Thank you. Um, we've had just a start from Andy and then Melissa. Oh, Look, I was just gonna go back to, um, just quickly just looking at the functions of the various emergency management committees, which are proposed under the bill. One of them talks about, um, that the responsibility is to ensure that sustainably, sorry, ensure that suitably trained and competent personnel, including volunteers that are available and there's an appropriate organizational structure for those personnel for the effective emergency management in its area.

And then it also has a, a monitoring and report on compliance for this area. So that would tend to suggest to me that there is a requirement for the emergency management committees to know that there's a voluntary component of this community component and to monitor that. Does that seem like a strong enough sort of arrangement to say, actually you can't just ignore the community in preparing your emergency plan?

No, but I think there is something about making it a bit easier, perhaps. Uh, we've had to reach out to five different Wellington networks and five national providers individually to develop this model that's just trying to connect all the different networks and organizations that are, we're not doing anything different other than trying to make the most of what already exists and make that clear to residents how they can be part of that.

Yeah. Okay. No, thank You. Um, Melissa, um, I just wanted to put this, if I happen to be a resident, I imagine it's, it's, it's, it's an if and I don't speak English. I'm a new migrant to New Zealand. I live with an elderly parent who speaks no English. How would they actually engage with you and what would you have actually done to me or to my family?

Great question. In the last couple of months that you are actually sort of, you know, planning all of this. Yes. So how would you have engaged and what would I have actually benefited from it? Right. There's a few questions there. Sorry. What we are hoping to is initially gather 200 people from different ethnicities and, and, and cultures who love gathering people, and they gather them into groups.

And we wrap a whole lot of support around those 200, what we calling community connectors, professional supervision, mentoring, support, an that, that helps 'em navigate, um, quarterly training, quarterly catch ups. So we want to love and support those 200 community connectors, and I'm really, really hoping they're across all the ethnicities.

Yeah. What I'm actually saying is that what would I have actually gotten out of it to prepare for, prepare me for an emergency that could actually happen, that could be a disaster. Okay. So by this, by September, 2026, we would hope to have all those groups connected so they all know each other and they all know how they can help their neighbors to meet local needs with local offers and whichever part of Newlands that is.

Thank you. Practically. Yeah. Well, We've run out of time, sadly, but That's right. Yeah. That's, um, really interesting information. I can give a real practical example in 15 seconds. Okay. Yep. So Jim and Jill are running the neighbor, the neighborhood group, and a part of Newlands. Um, um, Andre comes running over saying his wife Andrea is under a concrete block bleeding out after the earthquake.

What I need my, the community connectors to know is we are the local builder, is that got the earth weaving equipment and where the local first data is. It is not good enough, from my perspective to have the best will in the world, but unable to help someone. Mm-hmm. Great. Does that make it, yeah. Really helpful. Thank you so much for your submission and for, um, appearing in front of us today and answering your question.
Thank you. We really appreciate it. Our next submitter is local government New Zealand, who are joining us on Zoom.
Thank You. Thank you.

Camera. Okay. Uh, just, so who was it? Local government, uh, ley. Oh, Ainsley. Welcome. Uh, thank you very much for your, uh, written submission, which we've got a copy of. Uh, we've got 10 minutes to hear from you and, uh, keen to, keen to hear, uh, if you've got any additional comments to make.

And, and if you wish, you can leave some time for questions over to you. Great. Thank you. Um, yeah, we were supposed to be joined by, um, M ltz, um, to do the, OR submission today, but unfortunately it doesn't seem like she's online. So I'll just, um, Team there is anyone else?
We'll, just checking, um, ley just to see If she's there. She should write in the chat room.
Oh, just coming on now.

Oh, welcome. Um, sorry about that. Um, good. Um, thank you for having us today. For those of you that dunno me, my name is ltz. I am the president of local government New Zealand, and, and Ainsley will be joining us today. Um, you would be well aware and just like we see here in the Gisborne region, that we are seeing more frequent and severe weather events that are affecting communities way more regularly than what we used to see.

And we need an emergency management system that is ready to meet this escalating risk. And we welcome the emergency management bill. It provides clearer roles, stronger coordination, and also a more consistent national approach, which is so, um, needed.

Um, but we need to be honest about what is happening in local government currently. There's a lot of pressure if you think of all the reforms happening. Uh, I, we are worried that the system cannot deliver on the bill's, um, um, ambition. So I'll talk a little bit. I've just got three areas that I would like to highlight, and you've received our written submission.

We do support a strong modern emergency management system, but we must be honest about how we are going to pay for it. Um, the, this bill increases expectations on councils at the same time as we are facing multiple overlapping reforms, each adding new responsibilities as well as costs.

Um, if we just use one example, like say the proposed rate caps, that will significantly limit council's ability to meet new obligations. And, um, I suspect many councils will simply not have the means to deliver what the bill requires. And this, uh, this creates a real risk for us that the bill's objectives will be undermined by uneven implementation, which is absolutely not what we want if we are looking for national consistency with some communities better protected than others.

And, um, one thing we want to highlight as well, that funding cannot continue to be reactive. Uh, we see it here in the Gisin region. Uh, currently we're still in a state of emergency. Um, we have support that are reactive. All of us arrives after a disaster. What we know what would work better is sustained upfront investment in resilience, hazard reduction, community capability, and also preparedness upfront investment pays off.

We know that it is much cheaper to invest before emergency happened so that when they happen there is less impact. Um, we're asking for a clearer commit, clearer commitment from central government to co-funding emergency management functions and resilience work, not just the response and recovery.

This will save New Zealand a significant money in the medium as well as long term. Next, I want to move to point number two is, uh, recognizing iwi Maori in communities. We've all seen Mara open their doors in the last three weeks. I know currently, um, there are still people living in emergency housing at Mara in our region.

Um, and we want to make sure Iwi is across and part of that decision making. And we do support the bill steps towards representation on the kicks or the co coordinating executives groups and planning for Office of as assistance. But these mechanisms do not yet reflect the scale of consistency of iwi and community in, uh, involvement across all phases, which is the readiness, which is very important.

Then the response, and then moving on to recovery. Um, we suspect without strongest structural support and resourcing engagement will remain uneven and also overly reliant on voluntary effort, which isn't fair, and it is also not sustainable.

Then the last point I would like to speak to is regulations can't be one size fits all. Um, we support national minimum standards to support consistency and clarity across the new system. Councils vary enormously in size, geography, what their hazard profiles are, and also the capacity.

Um, what is reasonable for a large metropolitan council may be totally unachievable for a small rural one. Um, standards must be risk-based, proportionate and flexible, not one size fits all. And I think council, we will all agree that councils need to be formally consulted on regulations to get this balance right.

Um, councils will also need support to implement those, um, practical guidelines, templates, targeted support, especially for our smaller and rural councils that lack that specialist staff. The cost that standards could shift onto councils, particularly those with low rate payer basis across large geographic areas, also will need to be supported with appropriate funding and some funding tools.

That's us. This is local government New Zealand. Just summarizing where we are at. And you do have our written submission, but I'm happy to answer questions. Um, if you have any. Thank you Mayor. Stats, we appreciate, um, the submission. I had a question, uh, to open up with around the, uh, issue that you raised around the definition of disproportionately affected communities.

Uh, in your written submission, you've, you've stated that it's a bit too broad and may create uncertainty for councils. And so I just wondered, um, I think it's sounds from your comments that you're generally supportive of the, you know, greater inclusion and making sure that the plans are, um, appropriate to the community. So what's a better way that we could, uh, assist councils, uh, through this bill?

If this, if in your view this definition is not, um, sufficiently clear, I might ask Ainsley to, to talk to that if she wants to, and then I can wrap it up. Thank you. Yeah. Um, yeah, I think maybe the definition itself could be a bit more principle based and include sort of a, um, a coverage across different communities that might expect it to be in that category.

Um, also some support and guidance around that for councils around what sort of communities, how they might be best reached. Um, that sort of thing could be valuable as well. Okay, thank you. That's, that's really helpful. We'll definitely look into that. Um, Andy Foster, Nice to, uh, nice to see you there. Um, just wanted to ask this question.

I've asked both the officials and the minister, the, the relationship between, um, the emergency management bill and, uh, resource management, uh, land use, uh, trying to avoid problems in the first place. Um, so we stopped building things that probably shouldn't be built in the wrong places. Um, do you think we've got that right? Do you think there's enough, um, enough link there in your view?
